segments.title: Furious becomes an aircraft carrier before Jutland
segments.summary: HMS Furious shifts from an impractical 18-inch-gun cruiser toward a flying-off ship as the Royal Navy recognizes aviation's value for spotting and reconnaissance.
segments.body: The Royal Navy was reconsidering Furious before the Battle of Jutland, not merely reacting to it afterward. Her speed and length offered room for aircraft at a time when spotting, reconnaissance, and counter-reconnaissance were becoming important fleet functions. The resulting forward flying-off deck was still an awkward compromise, but it turned an uncertain Fisher design into a practical test bed for carrier aviation.

evidence.note: The transcript traces Furious from Fisher's departure through the decision to replace her forward 18-inch gun with aviation facilities for spotting and reconnaissance.
The transcript explains the growing wartime importance of naval aviation and Japanese observation of British carrier trials.

segments.title: Argus tests the full-length flight deck
segments.summary: The conversion of the liner Conte Rosso into HMS Argus lets the Royal Navy compare bridge, island, and flush-deck arrangements through models and wind-tunnel tests.
segments.body: British interest in a continuous flight deck predated the war, but an available Italian liner hull made a working carrier possible. Designers tested a bridge spanning the deck, a starboard island, and a flush deck at the National Physical Laboratory, rejecting arrangements that produced dangerous airflow. Argus shows how wartime resources allowed the Royal Navy to build and compare competing carrier concepts instead of relying on paper studies alone.

evidence.note: The transcript connects prewar full-deck proposals to the purchase and conversion of the Italian liner Conte Rosso.
The transcript describes wind-tunnel testing of bridge, island, and flush-deck models and explains the operational tradeoffs.

segments.title: Hermes establishes the purpose-built carrier layout
segments.summary: Trials with Furious and Argus feed into HMS Hermes, whose starboard island and carrier-specific hull show the advantages of designing for aviation from the keel up.
segments.body: Furious exposed the turbulence and operating problems created by retaining a large central superstructure. Hermes instead placed the island to starboard, reflecting both airflow testing and pilots' tendency to turn left when escaping a bad approach. Her smaller hull carried aircraft more efficiently than converted ships, illustrating how purpose-built internal volume could matter more than displacement alone.

evidence.note: The transcript moves from Furious's turbulent central superstructure to Hermes's purpose-built starboard-island arrangement.
The transcript compares Hermes and Furious by speed, displacement, air group, and conversion efficiency.

segments.title: What if Fisher built three wartime carriers?
segments.summary: A counterfactual replaces Courageous, Glorious, and Furious with fast purpose-built carriers able to field a combined air group of roughly 180 aircraft in 1916.
segments.body: Fisher understood the value of aviation for reconnaissance, gunfire spotting, counter-reconnaissance, and eventually strike, yet chose large guns for the three ships. Building them on the emerging Hermes model could have produced three fast carriers with about sixty aircraft apiece. The scenario highlights the gap between recognizing a new technology's promise and accepting the institutional risk of designing a fleet around it.

evidence.note: The transcript estimates that three purpose-built Courageous-class carriers could have fielded roughly 180 aircraft by late 1916.
The transcript contrasts proven heavy guns with still-developing aviation and describes Britain's parallel carrier experiments.

segments.title: Eagle shows how war and peace change carrier procurement
segments.summary: HMS Eagle turns a partly built battleship into a carrier, but debate over flush and island decks helps delay commissioning until 1924.
segments.body: The Royal Navy selected a hull whose machinery and structure were already advanced, then added the hangar, flight deck, and island required for aviation. Technical difficulty was not the only cause of delay: the end of wartime urgency forced the project to compete for peacetime funding and justify its layout. Eagle therefore illustrates how procurement tempo depends as much on institutions and budgets as on shipyard capability.

evidence.note: The transcript explains why the Royal Navy chose the incomplete battleship hull and debated flush-deck versus island layouts.
The transcript contrasts wartime necessity with peacetime justification, cost analysis, and slower funding.

segments.title: Hermes carries aviation infrastructure overseas
segments.summary: Hermes is presented as a cruiser or presence carrier that brings aircraft support to stations where the Royal Navy cannot afford permanent aviation bases.
segments.body: At 25 knots, Hermes was not conceived as a later high-speed fleet carrier. Her role grew from wartime experience off Africa and at Gallipoli, where aircraft proved useful but depended on scarce shore infrastructure. Deployments to the China Station and Mediterranean made the carrier a mobile package of aircraft, maintenance, supplies, and operating support.

evidence.note: The transcript defines Hermes as a presence carrier carrying aviation infrastructure to distant stations.

segments.title: Hōshō reflects an early support-carrier doctrine
segments.summary: British trial data helps move Hōshō from a seaplane-carrier concept to a flush-deck carrier, while her reduced speed reveals assumptions about operating behind the battle fleet.
segments.body: Japanese designers used British experience to abandon an initial flying-off and seaplane arrangement, then removed Hōshō's island after observing airflow problems on Furious and Argus. The decision to reduce her design speed to about 25 knots followed an early belief that carriers would stand off and support the battle fleet rather than maneuver as its striking center. Later British exercises showed how vulnerable that separation could make an unescorted carrier.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Hōshō's evolution from seaplane-support ideas to a purpose-built flush-deck carrier.
The transcript links her reduced speed to support-carrier assumptions and contrasts them with British exercise experience.

segments.title: Langley and Furious turn experiments into carrier doctrine
segments.summary: USS Langley and the repeatedly rebuilt Furious demonstrate why navies needed ships, exercises, and operating experience before they could form mature carrier doctrine.
segments.body: Langley gave the US Navy a converted platform for experimenting with wheeled aircraft, seaplanes, and the practical limits imposed by a 15-knot hull. Furious likewise accumulated compromises through repeated rebuilding, including poor visibility and difficult flight operations, yet remained useful from the 1918 Tondern raid to the 1944 attack on Tirpitz. Their long service preserved valuable capacity, but treaties and scarce new construction also slowed the replacement cycle that would have converted lessons into better purpose-built carriers and fleet doctrine.

evidence.note: The transcript traces Langley's institutional origins, mixed aviation facilities, and speed limitations as an experimental carrier.
The transcript explains Furious's final layout, flight-operation visibility problems, and the need for exercises before doctrine could mature.
The transcript follows Furious from First World War aircraft to Barracudas and argues that treaty-era limits slowed experimental replacement.
